title: Rum and Coca-Cola
lore: Working for the Yankee dollar. Louis Prima's 1944 read of the Calypso hit — wartime swing with a wink.

title: (I've Got Spurs That) Jingle Jangle Jingle
lore: A free, fancy, footloose cowboy tune from 1942 — later beamed across the Mojave on New Vegas radio.

title: Stormy Weather
lore: Arlen & Koehler's torch standard — "don't know why there's no sun up in the sky." A 1946 dance-band reading of a Cotton Club classic.

title: A Dream Is a Wish Your Heart Makes
lore: Perry Como's velvet read of the Cinderella waltz — "a dream is a wish your heart makes when you're fast asleep." 1950, the year it became the country's lullaby.

title: Into Each Life Some Rain Must Fall
lore: Bill Kenny and Ella trade verses on the weather of the heart. Decca 23356, 1944 — heartbreak you can slow-dance to.

title: Pennsylvania 6-5000
lore: Glenn Miller's swing tribute to a Manhattan phone number — the whole band shouting "Pennsylvania six-five-oh-oh-oh!" 1940, pure pre-war jukebox joy.

title: In the Blue Evening
lore: A young Frank Sinatra out front of the Tommy Dorsey band, 1943 — the bobby-sox idol before the wasteland, crooning into the blue.

title: Don't Sit Under the Apple Tree
lore: Glenn Miller, Tex Beneke and the Modernaires lay down the home-front promise — "with anyone else but me." 1942, sweethearts shipping out.

title: Dream a Little Dream of Me
lore: The 1931 standard rendered on a player-piano roll by Lawrence Cook — phantom fingers, no pianist in the room. The old world dreaming itself.

title: All of Me
lore: Before the Rat Pack, before Vegas — a young Dino with Nat Brandwynne's orchestra, c.1946, asking you to take all of him. The New Vegas sound, a decade early.

title: It's a Sin to Tell a Lie
artist: Frank "Big Boy" Goodie et son Orchestre
lore: Billy Mayhew's standard cut in Paris by expatriate hornman Frank "Big Boy" Goodie — Hot Club swing with a wagging finger, 1939.

title: I Got the Sun in the Morning
lore: Dean Martin swings Irving Berlin's "I got the sun in the morning and the moon at night" — 1946, all easy postwar optimism.

title: Heartaches
lore: The 1931 torch song a 1947 reissue turned into a surprise smash — heartaches by the number, drifting up from Galaxy News Radio territory.

title: Civilization (Bongo, Bongo, Bongo)
lore: "Bongo, bongo, bongo, I don't want to leave the Congo" — postwar irony that aged into wasteland gallows humor.

title: A Wonderful Guy
lore: Nellie Forbush's giddy declaration from South Pacific — "I'm in love with a wonderful guy." Joe Loss's British dance-band take, 1949.

title: Sentimental Journey
lore: "Gonna take a sentimental journey, gonna set my heart at ease." The 1945 homecoming anthem — the war was ending and everyone was going home.

title: Butcher Pete (Part 1)
lore: Roy Brown's jump-blues about a butcher "hackin' all over town" — a 1949 double-entendre romp later spun on Fallout 4's Diamond City Radio.

title: I Don't Want to Set the World on Fire
lore: Fallout's signature lullaby for the end of everything. The Ink Spots, Decca Studios, August 1941.

title: Diane
artist: Buddy Lucas & His Band of Tomorrow
lore: Rapée & Pollack's 1928 waltz reborn as a honking 1951 R&B instrumental by Buddy Lucas's Band of Tomorrow — smoky, slow, after-hours.

title: Blue Moon
lore: Rodgers & Hart's lonesome standard smoothed into a 1948 cocktail-trio reverie — "you saw me standing alone."

title: A String of Pearls
lore: Jerry Gray's glide for the Miller band — November 1941, a month before everything changed. Pure ballroom shimmer.

title: From the Bottom of My Heart
lore: Sinatra fronting the Harry James orchestra — "From the Bottom of My Heart," 1945. The voice that would outlive the bombs.

title: Pistol Packin' Mama
lore: Lay that pistol down, babe. Crosby and the Andrews Sisters, Decca 23277, 1943 — a jukebox staple in every diner still standing.

title: I'll Be Seeing You
lore: Kahal & Fain's parting standard — "I'll be seeing you in all the old familiar places." 1944, the song that meant goodbye for a generation.

title: Anything Goes
lore: Cole Porter's catalog of a world gone topsy-turvy — "the world's gone mad today." Jack Payne's 1935 band still sounds like a prophecy.
